one_line_takeaway: Attackers can poison Android malware detectors without touching a single label, by tricking VirusTotal's AV engines into mislabeling benign apps carrying an inert malicious signature hidden in res/ -- flipping a chosen app to 'malware' with as few as 5 samples (0.015% of training data), and existing anomaly-detection defenses fail.
key_finding: AV-based crowd-sourced labeling is a poisoning vector that current feature extractors and defenses cannot stop. Real uploads confirm feasibility: all 160 submitted spoofed APKs were flagged malicious (avg ~21+/-2.9 AV detections, >13 in absolute terms), while 20,284 merely-repackaged (un-injected) APKs triggered ZERO detections, isolating the injection as the cause. DoS: only 1% poisoning degrades detectors, and the integrity attack flips the specific TikTok app to 'malware' with just 5 clones (0.015% of the training set) while global accuracy is preserved. The Deep-KNN defense fails: poisoned samples cluster together (all carry the attack-desired malicious label), so KNN cannot isolate them, and the defense even harms clean utility (e.g. GBT TPR drops ~20% at 0.1% FPR, k=11).
core_contribution: The paper introduces 'label spoofing' attacks and AndroVenom, a methodology that poisons ML-based Android malware detectors WITHOUT the attacker ever labeling anything directly. Instead of the usual clean/dirty/label-flip settings, the attacker coerces the crowd-sourced labeling oracle (VirusTotal's AV engines) into mislabeling. The attacker takes a benign APK, injects a small known-malicious file (recognized by AV signatures via its cryptographic hash) into the res/ folder, repackages it, and uploads it to VirusTotal, where AV engines flag it malicious. When downstream practitioners scrape such samples as 'malware' into training sets, the ML detector is poisoned. Two attack modes are demonstrated: (a) Denial-of-Service (availability) that degrades overall detector accuracy, and (b) integrity attacks that flip a specific benign app (TikTok) to 'malware' while keeping global accuracy intact. This is the paper's OWN novel framing; the related-work table (Table XI) surveys prior feature-space/problem-space poisoning attacks but none exploit the AV-annotation trust chain.
threat_model: Attacker capability is deliberately weak/realistic: they can repackage benign APKs and submit them to a crowd-sourcing platform (VirusTotal), but they CANNOT directly set training labels. They exploit the blind trust practitioners place in AV aggregate labels. The injected file (e.g. an EICAR-like known-malicious sample) adds no malicious functionality at runtime; it only carries a signature that AVs match. Injection targets the res/ folder specifically because state-of-the-art static feature extractors (Drebin, MaMaDroid) ignore res/ content, so the poisoned sample's feature vector is unchanged, evading pre-processing filters. Attacker also chooses the malware family the sample is labeled as (via AVClass agreement). Adversary knowledge is effectively black-box w.r.t. the victim model; manipulation is problem-space (real APK files, not just feature vectors).
limitations: Stated by the authors: (1) injection inevitably modifies the APK and invalidates its digital signature, so an AV vendor could allow-list legitimate signed apps to blunt production integrity attacks; (2) they inject whole files recognizable by their hash rather than the minimal indispensable modification, leaving stealth improvements open; (3) developer-side integrity checks or dynamic analysis could expose the tampering since only static features are considered; (4) real uploads were intentionally limited to 160 samples to avoid contaminating VirusTotal, so the at-scale annotation effect is inferred/simulated; (5) the study offers no strong remediation, only suggesting richer feature sets that include res/ resources and clustering/ensemble defenses as future work; the finding is also somewhat specific to the Android/APK format.

datasets_used: Ruggia et al. replicated dataset (downloaded from VirusTotal by hash): 20,329 malicious APKs spanning 196 families (100-114 samples/family) and 20,769 benign APKs across 50 Google Play Store categories
Dl: large-scale balanced dataset of 40K samples (equal malware/goodware)
Ds: small-scale balanced dataset of 1K samples (500 benign + 500 malicious)
Repack-able Goodware Dataset: 20,284 benign APKs that repackage without error
10 injected malicious files (Table I), one per top-MIME-type (e.g. text/xml 'groooboor' 316 B 29/62 detections; app/x-sharedlib 'lotoor' 4505 B 33/65)
quantitative_results: 97.7% (20,284/20,769) of benign APKs repackage correctly with Apktool
Injection increases APK size by only 2-3% on average (median ~1.4%)
All 160 spoofed APKs uploaded to VirusTotal flagged malicious, avg detection ~21+/-2.9 (>13 absolute); 20,284 repackaged-but-uninjected APKs got 0 detections
DoS small-scale (Ds, 1% poison, 0.1% FPR): TPR drop 5.9% LSVM, 5.1% GBT, 20.4% NN vs poison-free baseline; NN falls below 50% TPR; GBT most robust (16.3% TPR drop at 1% FPR even with 20% poison)
DoS large-scale (Dl, 0.1% FPR): ~10% TPR drop reached with 1643 (LSVM), 3287 (GBT), 328 (NN) poisoned APKs out of 32K training; MaMaDroid RF ~7% drop
DoS FPR@95%TPR (Drebin, Dl): GBT FPR rises 0.6%->1.0%->1.4%->3.0% at 5/10/20% poison; Pearson corr(FPR, poison ratio) >0.95 (p<0.05); RF@90%TPR rises 1.9%->2.9%

Integrity (target TikTok): 5 variants (0.015% of training) misclassify TikTok for all 3 Drebin classifiers at 1% FPR; MaMaDroid RF -> 100% ASR at 1% FPR and 90% ASR at 0.1% FPR with 5 variants; global TPR fluctuates <=1-2% (1% FPR) and <=5-10% (0.1% FPR)
Deep-KNN defense (k=11, 0.1% FPR): NN TPR +20% but GBT TPR -20%; for MaMaDroid RF the defense collapses clean TPR (58.9%->12.1% at k=11) and does not stop integrity attacks (ASR stays 100% at 1% FPR)
baselines_compared: Poison-free (0% poisoning) baseline for every model/feature/FPR configuration
Deep KNN anomaly-detection defense (Peri et al., k=3 and k=11) as the sole tested countermeasure, plus CLN+DEF control on clean data

Feature extractors compared: Drebin vs MaMaDroid; models: LSVM (lambda=1), GBT (300/3000 trees), 5-layer NN (24000-240-120-60-1, Drebin dims 732,800 reduced to 24,000 via random projection), Random Forest (300 trees, MaMaDroid)

Table XI survey of 14 prior poisoning attacks (Chen 2018, Demontis 2019, Severi 2021, GTA 2021, Jigsaw Puzzle 2023, etc.) contextualizing AndroVenom as a problem-space, AV-annotation-exploiting attack
